title: 3 3 Grad Div Curl
body:
3.3 GRADIENT, DIVERGENCE & CURL

For any scalar field φ(x, y, z) and a vector field A(x, y, z) = A1 i + A2 j + A3 k,

curl (grad φ) = ∇ × (∇φ) = 0 curl of gradient

A vector field F(x, y, z) that satisfies ∇ × F = 0 is called an irrotational or curl-free vector field. A curl-free vector field can always be written in terms of a scalar potential field φ(x, y, z) such that

F = ∇φ ⇐⇒ ∇ × F = 0

Physical Interpretation of div F = ∇ · F The divergence of a vector field tells us the rate at which the flow represented by the vector field diverges (positive divergence) or converges (negative divergence) at a particular point. The divergence also tells us something about the presence of sources or sinks in the flow. Examples of divergent and convergent vector fields are shown in Fig 1.

F=xi+yj+zk F = -x i - y j - z k

z 1 z 0 0 -1 -1 -2 -2 2 2 2 2 0 0 0 0 y -2 -2 y -2 -2 x x

Figure 1: (Left) Positive divergence. (Right) Negative divergence (convergence).

Imagine that F is a vector field representing the flow of water. Let us now imagine that we place a small wire cube into the fluid so that water can flow through any of its six faces. We want to measure the rate at which water flows out of the cube. Clearly, if we make the cube larger then the rate of flow out of the cube will also be larger. So we will calculate the rate of outflow per unit the volume of the cube, R.

First imagine what would happen if the water is moving at a constant speed in one direction, for example in a strongly flowing river. Then R = 0 because all the water entering the cube leaves at the same rate on the other side — there is no net outflow of water from the cube.

Now imagine that we place a strong source of water inside the cube, for example a hose. Then R will be positive because water leaves the cube on all of its faces. Alternatively, if we placed a sink of water inside the cube, for example a drain hole, then R will be negative because water is entering the cube on all of its faces.

Now we can relate this to the divergence as follows. Assume first that the flow is in the x-direction so that F = F1 (x, y, z)i. The flow through the left-hand face of the cube is given by F1 (x, y, z) so the rate of outflow through this face is −F1 (x, y, z) × ∆y∆z. Notice that the flow is negative because fluid is flowing into the cube on the left side. We have also multiplied by the area of the face, ∆y∆z.

The flow through the right-hand face of the cube is given by F1 (x + ∆x, y, z). So the rate of outflow through this face is F1 (x + ∆x, y, z) × ∆y∆z. It is positive because the flow is out of the cube on the right side. So the total rate of outflow per unit volume is

F1 (x, y, z)∆y∆z F1 (x + ∆x, y, z)∆y∆z F1 (x + ∆x, y, z) − F1 (x, y, z) R=− + = ∆x∆y∆z ∆x∆y∆z ∆x where we have divided by the volume of the cube, ∆x∆y∆z.

In the limit of the cube becoming very small, ∆x, ∆y and ∆z will go to zero. The rate of outflow per unit volume will then be

F1 (x + ∆x, y, z) − F1 (x, y, z) ∂F1 R = lim∆x→0 = ∆x ∂x

We can repeat this process in each direction for a vector field F = F1 i + F2 j + F3 k. Then the net outflow of water on all six sides is ∂F1 ∂F2 ∂F3 R= + + = ∇ · F. ∂x ∂y ∂z

Example 1 Consider the vector field F = x2 i + y 2 j + 0k. Calculate the divergence of F and show in the x − y plane where the vector field has positive, negative, and zero divergence.

F ∇ · F = 2x + 2y. F

Physical Interpretion of curl F = ∇ × F The curl(F) measures infinitesimal rotations in the vector field F(x, y, z). Imagine that we place a tiny sphere in the flow at the point P . We let the sphere’s centre be fixed at P but we allow it to rotate in any direction. The curl(F) is a vector with direction along the axis of rotation and magnitude equal to the rate of rotation. The direction of the vector is given by the right-hand screw rule. If you hold your right hand so that your fingers curl in the direction of rotation, then your thumb will point along the axis of rotation in the direction of the vector curl(F). Examples of rotating vector fields are shown in Fig 2.

F = -y i + x j F=yi-xj

2 2

1 1 z z 0 0

-1 -1

-2 -2 2 2 2 2 0 0 0 0 y -2 -2 y -2 -2 x x

Figure 2: (Left) Anticlockwise rotation about the z-axis. (Right) Clockwise rotation.

To see the connection between the curl and the rotation rate, consider again a tiny cube placed in a flow given by the vector field F(x, y, z). Let us consider the rotation rate about the z-axis caused by the action of the flow on the sides of the cube. To calculate the rotation rate Ωz , we will first calculate the circulation of the flow around the cube and then divide by the cross-sectional area of the cube.

The circulation of water around the cube will be given by the velocity of the flow along each side multiplied by the length of that side. Thus we have, moving in an anticlockwise direction

circulation = F1 (x, y, z)∆x+F2 (x+∆x, y, z)∆y−F1 (x+∆x, y+∆y, z)∆x−F2 (x, y+∆y, z)∆y

Notice that the contributions to the circulation along two of the sides are negative because the flow is directed in a clockwise direction on these sides. Dividing by the cross-sectional area of the cube, ∆x∆y then gives the rotation rate about the z-axis

F2 (x + ∆x, y, z) − F2 (x, y + ∆y, z) F1 (x + ∆x, y + ∆y, z) − F1 (x, y, z) Ωz = − ∆x ∆y Taking the limit of ∆x, ∆y, ∆z → 0 then gives ∂F2 ∂F1 Ωz = − . ∂x ∂y Again, we can repeat this calculation for the rotation rates about the x-axis and y-axis to give all three components ∂F3 ∂F2 ∂F1 ∂F3 ∂F2 ∂F1 Ωx = − , Ωy = − , Ωz = − ∂y ∂z ∂z ∂x ∂x ∂y We can now form a new vector of the rotation rates around each axis, Ω = Ωx i+Ωy j+Ωz k. This vector is exactly the curl of the vector field F.

i j k ∂ ∂ ∂ Ω= ∂x ∂y ∂z = ∇ × F. F1 F2 F3

Example 2 Consider the vector field F = y 2 i+x2 j+0k. Calculate the curl of F. Notice that this vector field has no k component, so it can only cause rotation in the x − y plane, that is, around the z-axis. This means that the curl will only have a k component.

Show in the x − y plane where the vector field has positive, negative, and zero curl.

F ∇ × F = (2x − 2y) k. F

Curl-free vector fields We close with an important identity relating the gradient and curl.

Theorem (curl of gradient): ∇ × (∇φ) = 0.

Proof: Let φ(x, y, z) be a scalar field.

A vector field with zero curl is called an irrotational or curl-free vector field. The gradient is an example of a curl-free vector field. The above identity implies that any vector field F(x, y, z) with zero curl can be expressed as the gradient of some scalar field φ(x, y, z), ∇×F=0 ⇐⇒ F = ∇φ. We call φ(x, y, z) the scalar potential for the curl-free vector field F(x, y, z).

An example of a curl-free vector field is the gravitational force. The gravitational force can be expressed as the gradient of the gravitational potential energy. We will see in the next lecture that the curl-free property makes it very easy to calculate the work done on a particle to move it through a force field — it is just the change in the potential energy. You can now do Q52,53,55
